6.5.1                    Resonance             in                              RLC              circuit.

Consider the dependence of the amplitude of the current I in an RLC circuit on the fre-
quency of the applied voltage ω.



                                                                  213

                           3.2.                       Geometrical                 representation                 of                 complex                 numb ers



                                   I =   V0| Z| =                    V0pR2+(ωL−(ωC)−1)2.


   where V0 is the amplitude of the applied volatge and Z = R+ j(ωL−(ωC)−1) is the
impedance of the circuit.

   The function I(ω) is non-monotonic. It has a maximum at a certain value ofωc that can
be found from


         dI
         dω = 0=(−0.5)                      V0(R2+(ωL−(ωC)−1)2)3/2      2(ωL−(ωC)−1)(L+    1Cω2)                 .


   Solving for ω, we obtain


                                           ω2c =   1LC,→ ωc =     1√LC.


   In order to visualize the function| I | (ω) we set L = C = 1 and plot| I | (ω) in the
unitsof V0 forthreedifferentvaluesoftheresistance R=(1,0.5,0.3), asshowninFig.6.12.

   Each of the three lines in Fig.6.12 has a maximum at ωc = 1.  As the resistance R de-
creases, the maximal value of the current I(ωc) increases. In the limit, when the resistance
of the circuit is negligibly small, i.e. R→ 0, the maximal value of the current diverges


                                            lim                    V0
                                           R→0I(ωc)= limR→0         R =∞.

   This phenomenon is called resonance and the critical frequency ωc = (LC)−1/2 is called
the resonance frequency of the circuit.


6.6                Parallel                   RLC           circuit


Consider an RLC circuit, where all three elements are connected in parallel, as shown in
Fig.6.13.

   In this case, the voltage drops across the resistor, the capacitor and the inductor are
identical and equal to the voltage at the source V(t)= V0ejωt


                                             VR = VC = VL = V(t).



                                                          214

                            3.2.                       Geometrical                 representation                 of                 complex                 numb ers












                               Figure 6.13: RLC cicuit connected in parallel.


   The respective complex currents IR, IC and IL are found from


                                         IR =  VZR,  IC =  VZC,  IL =  VZL,


   where the complex impedances ZR, ZC and ZL are given by


                                       ZR = R,  ZC =    1jωC,   ZL = ωLj.


   Conservation of current implies that the current through the source IS is equal to the
sum of IR, IC and IL


                                                  IS = IR+ IC+ IL.

   Writing I as I =  VZ, where Z is the complex impedance of the parallel circuit, we obtain


                                            IS = VZ =  VZR +  VZC +  VZL.


   Consequently

                   1
                  Z =   1ZR +  1ZC +  1ZL =  1R− ωCj   +   1jωL =  1R + j                ωC−   1ωL        .

This shows that the current through the source IS is

                                            q
                                  IS = V0      R−2+(ωC−(ωL)−1)2ej(ωt+ϕ),

   where the phase shift ϕ between the voltage V(t) and the current IS is




                                                            215

                            3.2.                       Geometrical                 representation                 of                 complex                 numb ers


















               Figure 6.14:Anti-resonance in the         RLC cicuit connected in parallel.



                                       ϕ= arg        1/R+ j        ωC−   1ωL           .


   The dependence of the amplitude of the current IS on the frequency ω is shown in
Fig.6.14.

   Remarkably, the magnitude of the current IS has a minimum, rather than a maximum
at the resonance frequency ωc = 1/√CL. Indeed from

            dIS
             dω =                         V02(R−2+(ωC−(ωL)−1)2)1/2 2(ωC−(ωL)−1)(C+   1ω2L)                    = 0


   we obtain the frequency at the minimum of IS


                                                       ωc =     1√CL.


   The RLC circuit connected in parallel is called anti-resonator.


6.7                Complex           rotation


Consider the operation of rotation of a complex number z = x+ jy by the angle α anti-
clockwise.   Under the rotation, the modulus |z| =px2+y2 = ρ remains unchanged,
while the argument arg(z) increases by α. Using the exponential form for z we obtain




                                                             216

                           3.2.                       Geometrical                 representation                 of                 complex                 numb ers



                                 z= ρejθ → z′ = ρej(θ+α) = ejαρejθ = ejαz

   Let us construct a two-dimensional real vector

                                                     ℜ(z)              x
                                             ⃗v=      ℑ(z)       =     y


   We are looking to derive the transformation rule for⃗v under the rotation by the angleα
anti-clockwise. Using the Euler’s formula, we obtain


                         z′ = x′+ jy′ = ejα(x+ jy)=(cosα+ jsinα)(x+ jy),
therefore,
                                z′ = xcosα−ysinα+ j(xsinα+ycosα).

   Therefore

                       ℜ(z)              x                   x′           cosα  −sinα              x
               ⃗v=      ℑ(z)       =     y     →⃗v′ =        y′     =     sinα      cosα            y     .


   Consequently, under the operation of rotation by angle α anti-clockwise, the real and
imaginary parts of z transform as components of a two-dimensional vector, in agreement
with Eq.(4.78).


Example 6.16 Find the image of z= 1+ j under the rotation byπ/2 clockwise.

   Solution


              z→ z′ = e−jπ/2z=(cosπ/2− jsinπ/2)(1+ j)=−j(1+ j)= 1− j.

   As we see, the rotation by π/2 clockwise corresponds to the multiplication by −j.  Similarly,
rotation byπ/2 anti-clockwise corresponds to the multiplication by j.

                                 √                                                                        2
                           =        3−1+ j(√√        3+1)
                                                2              .

Therefore
                                                         √3−1+ j(√3+1)                        √3−1+ j(√3+1)
       z′→ z′′ = ejπz′ =(cosπ+ jsinπ)                                √2             =−                    √2              .


6.8                Di erential           equations:               complex           method           of           solution


In many applications in physics and engineering, one needs to solve a vector linear differ-
ential equation of the form

                                             d⃗P
                                              dt =−  1ϵ0χ⃗P+⃗Eext(t),                                                  (6.121)

where⃗P = Pxi+Pyj is a two-dimensional solution vector and⃗Eext(t) = Ex(t)i+Ey(t)j is
an external time-dependent forcing.

   Such equation appears, for example, in elctrostatics, when calculating the response of a
dielectricmaterialtoatime-dependentexternalelectricfield⃗Eext(t). Here⃗Pispolarization
density vector of the dielectric medium, ϵ0 is the electric permutivity of vacuum, χ is the
dielectric susceptibility of the medium.

   Consider the response of the medium to the so-called circularly polarized external elec-
tric field ⃗Eext(t) = E0cosωt⃗i+ E0sinωt⃗j, where E0 is the amplitude of the field and ω
is the rotation frequency. From Eq.(6.121), the components of the polarization density ⃗P
vector satisfy

                                             ˙Px   =  −  1ϵ0χPx+E0cosωt,

                                             ˙Py   =  −  1ϵ0χPy+E0sinωt.                                          (6.122)

In order to solve Eq.(6.122), we employ the complex method of solution and introduce a
complex-valued function

                                              Z(t)= Px(t)+ jPy(t).                                                   (6.123)





                                                               218

                           3.2.                       Geometrical                 representation                 of                 complex                 numb ers

If we add the two equations in Eq.(6.122), we obtain

                       ˙Px+ j ˙Py   =  −Px+ jPyϵ0χ    +E0ejωt,→  ˙Z =−  Zϵ0χ +Ec(t),                (6.124)


where Ec is the complex external forcing Ec(t)= E0(cosωt+ jsinωt)= E0ejωt.

   Complex differential equation Eq.(6.124) can be solved using the standard method for
real-valued functions. Namely, the general solution is given by

                                    Z(t)= Cexp          −  tϵ0χ    + Aejωt,                                           (6.125)

where C is an arbitrary complex number and the complex constant A can be determined
by substituting Eq.(6.125) into Eq.(6.124). Thus we obtain

                                      jωAejωt =−Aejωtϵ0χ  +E0ejωt.                                              (6.126)

Consequently

                                            A=           E0jω+(ϵ0χ)−1.                                                      (6.127)


   This shows that in the long-time limit, when t → ∞, the response of the dielectric
medium to the circularly polarized electric field is given by


                                       t→∞Z(t)=        E0ejωtlimjω+(ϵ0χ)−1.                                               (6.128)


The physical components of the polarization vector ⃗P can be found from Eq.(6.128) by
taking the real and imaginary parts


                 t→∞Px(t)=  E0cos(ωt−ϕ)limp               t→∞Py(t)=  E0sin(ωt−ϕ)p
                                     ω2+(ϵ0χ)−2,   lim                        ω2+(ϵ0χ)−2,                 (6.129)

where the phase shift ϕ= tan−1(ωϵ0χ).

   Eqs.(6.129)demonstratethatthemaximalpolarizationamplitudemax(Px)= max(Py)=
ϵ0χE0 corresponds to a time-independent forcing, i.e. ω→ 0. Polarization amplitude de-
creases monotonically withω and the vector⃗P forms the angleϕ= tan−1(ωϵ0χ) with the
external circularly polarized forcing⃗Eext.
